speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6. Kaethe Wells.

speaker: interviewer
text: After liberation, did you see or know or meet any famous or infamous people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very friendly with some of the American occupation. Some of my former school friends found us. One was with the CIC-- CIC, the other was with OSS. And my mother, really such a kind and gentle soul, but the hate in her heart, especially for this one man in-- from Florsheim, Schtamm was his name. He was the one who had sent my father to Buchenwald first, and his wife was the one who brought the little tiny Nazi girls to our house at the-- the day after the Kristallnacht and said, you see what happens to the Jews? They should all be killed or sent to Palestine at that time. The-- our friends from the CIC came one day and said, we have a man someplace-- I think it was Hofheim-- yeah, it was Hofheim-- in prison. You have to come and identify him and then-- what do you call it-- see that he gets prison term. So Mother and I went, and that was the one and only time. We never did it again. She had another stroke after that. Went to prison, the minute she saw him, oh, my god. It was a good thing he was inside and we were outside. I immediately took her out and I said yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- [LAUGHS] I was already in America when I had a letter from my sister, said, Mother had another little stroke because the wife, the same person who came and brought the little children to our house, came to my mother and said, please, help my husband get out of prison. My mother slammed the door in her face, of course. But the nerve? How did the Holocaust experience affect you in your new life after liberation with trust, with love, with-- with getting feelings again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I really wanted [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] was to get married and have a child. [SOBBING] And I think that when I had my first baby-- my mother was here, she came for that-- I was so grateful, but I also was so afraid that something would happen to her, that I think I overdid the Jewish mother. And I really spoiled her and just nothing should ever happen to her. I made a terrible mistake. When I had my second child, my son, I didn't do it. And I-- he grew up-- they both grew up beautifully. But I was just grateful and thankful, healthy children, wonderful loving children, a marriage 47 years. We already had planned our 50th anniversary. I'm a very lucky girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start feeling again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think it took a long time after I was married. But when my husband realized that fear I had, you know, looking over shoulder, he-- he helped me. He helped me with it. And just-- just the normal American life. All I ever-- I never told-- I wanted to tell my children about the Holocaust, but I didn't because I wanted my children to grow up typical American children, not the Germans. I mean, we were brought up strict. Ay yai yai yai yai, strict. I wanted them to be Americans, and I did not want this to hang over their heads, the past of their mother. I don't-- I didn't want them to worry. I never had a youth. I was never a teenager. I was a child one day, and the next day I was 60 years old. I wanted my children to experience the freedom and the happiness and the silliness and all this of being a-- a-- a child and a teenager. And through them I relived it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever lose your faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: My faith? Oh, yes. I certainly did. At that time, when my father was arrested for the last time, I had quite a talk, and I said you are not there. And my faith came back-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, I was already here. And I cannot put my finger on the day. But, oh, did it come back with bells and everything else, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're proud today to be a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my goodness. And my friend upstairs-- oh, I made a deal with him a long, long time ago. And he kept his side of the bargain, and I tell you, I'm trying very hard to-- I must keep my side of the bargain, because he's given me miracles, and he's shown me he is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think now would be a wonderful time to meet your family. No? Mrs. Wells, please introduce us to your daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Clara. We call her [? Clarchen, ?] the German name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a son, PJ, Philip Julius, and he was born-- two grandfathers, Philip and Julius, the nurse said, you're not going to let that little boy run through life Philip Julius. Let's call him PJ. He's been Dr. PJ ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a dentist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Canton, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, he's married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's married to a lovely girl, Fran Greenberger from Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katie and Jenny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are Katie and Jenny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katie is 14 and Jenny is 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Clara, do you feel differently than-- now that you see what your mother is doing, expressing everything that she has been through, than perhaps you did before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I feel any-- any differently. I'm so happy that this is being done, because it's always been very important to Mom to share her story and to-- so that this will not be forgotten. And as far as I'm concerned, I grew up, as Mother talked about a little bit, she didn't want to burden us, you know, with this story. She wanted us to have a happy, carefree childhood. I had questions, and I had curiosity, but my-- the message I gave myself was, it was too painful for her to talk about. And that was the reason she wasn't sharing anything with us, except for here and there. So I never asked any questions, because I didn't want to, you know, cause her any-- any unnecessary pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't until I was in my 30s that we found out what kind of communication, you know, gap we had there. And I really-- I have very little of that history. I have stories here and there. So I'm-- I'm-- I can't wait to get the tape. If there was one question that you had growing up that you wanted to ask your mother, what would it have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I guess-- that's a good question. I have never thought of that. I-- I guess it would have been more an emotional question in terms of, you know, how do you live with this? How did you live through it?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a good time to ask her.

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- I have to go back to that young Catholic priest in Buchenwald. You totally empty yourself and just-- you become an auto-- a robot. That's how you live through it. But then when you have two children, [LAUGHS] you can't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you knew in-- in many, many years from now that your great-great-grandchildren were going to view this tape, what would you like them to know about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did this tape-- I know it will be shown and hopefully it will help many people understand better. I know it will help my children understand better, but really what I want them to know is that their grandmother came to America and started, hopefully, a wonderful family. And this is my way of saying, hey, this is where you come from. Now live up to it, because the past, what we went through so that you can have all this. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yuck.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the card my father sent from concentration camp Buchenwald in November 1938. [PAUSES FOR 44 SECONDS] He wrote, dear wife and dear children, I'm sitting here-- when we read what he wrote, we all three of us decided that it is his handwriting-- forced handwriting. My father never wrote like this, but it is his handwriting. But somebody dictated to him, because this is not the way he speaks or writes. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] This is my mother, Liesel [? Rut ?] Schohl. She was 35 years old at the time. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] This is my father, 1937. He always wore his miniature medals from World War I. [PAUSES FOR 41 SECONDS] This is my husband, Herman, born and raised in Charleston. He loved West Virginia. He had automobile and toy store. And we were married 47 years. He passed away in 1994. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is my sister, Hela. I don't remember when this picture was taken. She sent it to me. She is still in Germany today, lives in Uberlingen, which is Lake Constance. She's married and has unfortunately become an invalid during the last few years. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my son, PJ. When he was born, two grandfathers, Philip and Julius, we had to name-- name him Philip Julius. But the nurse in the hospital thought that was terrible. So she named him PJ, and he has been stuck with that name ever since. He is a dentist in Canton, Ohio, married a wonderful girl, had two-- has two lovely daughters. Of course, he does. And he wanted to be here so bad, but he just simply could not come. Dr. PJ. We love him very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK. My three girls. My daughter-in-law, Fran, my oldest granddaughter, Katie, and my not so little one, Jenny. Katie had her bat mitzvah two years ago. And Jenny is going to have hers this August.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does your daughter-in-law do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a-- a teacher, special education teacher, in Canton.
